coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of orange-emissive silicon nanoparticles as “off-on” fluorescence probe for sensitive and selective detection of l-methionine and copper
coredata.dc:creator: Nsanzamahoro, Stanislas
Wang, Wei-Feng
Zhang, Ying
Shi, Yan-Ping
Yang, Jun-Li
coredata.dc:description:
Fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs) are of great interest as they are free of heavy ions. However, most of Si NPs exhibit blue or green emission, while orange or red-emitting Si NPs are required for an extensive range of applications. Copper ion (Cu2+) and l
-methionine (L-Met) detection is critically valuable point since their abnormal level is an indicator of various diseases. In this work, we illustrate an “off-on” method for sensitively and selectively determination of Cu2+ and L-Met using Si NPs as fluorescent probe. The Si NPs emitting orange fluorescence with the quantum yield of 2.23% were prepared via one and easy step of hydrothermal treatment of 3(2-aminoethylamino) propyl (dimethoxymethylsilane) (AEAPDMMS) and 2–aminophenol as precursors. The fluorescence of Si NPs was quenched in the presence of Cu2+ due to the strong metal-ligand coordination and electrostatic interactions between the large amount of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of Si NPs and Cu2+. Surprisingly, the resulted non-fluorescent Si NPs-Cu2+ complex displayed a fluorescence “turn-on” toward L-Met, due to the competitive coordination of Cu2+ between L-Met and Si NPs which leads to the unique “off-on” response to L-Met after the release of free Si NPs. The as-proposed approach is fast, simple, low cost and environmental-friendly. More importantly, it has been applied in the determination of Cu2+ and L-Met in water and urine samples, respectively with satisfactory recoveries. Furthermore, the approach could detect Cu2+ and L-Met with detection limit of 0.012 μM and 0.07 μM, which are lower than the level of Cu2+ in drinking water and of L-Met in human urine sample (maximum ~20 μM and ~5.9 μM, respectively).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon nanoparticles
Copper

l
-methionine
Turn “off-on”
Fluorescence
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensor preparation and its application for L-Met detection
L-Met and Cu2+ determination in real samples
Optimization of synthetic condition of Si NPs
Sensitivity of Si NPs towards Cu2+
Si NPs-Cu2+ as fluorescence turn-on sensor for L-Met
L-Met and Cu2+determination in actual samples

Outstanding Youth Fund of Gansu Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21705156 and 81673325 ), Outstanding Youth Fund of Gansu Province ( 20JR10RA061 ), CAS "Light of West China" Program, CAS Pioneer Hundred Talents Program, and the Chinese Academy of Sciences-the World Academy of Sciences (CAS- TWAS ) President's Fellowship Program.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (Inset: size distribution histogram) and (B) FT-IR spectra of Si NPs.

High resolution XPS spectra of Si NPs. (A) Full range, (B) C1s, (C) N1s, (D) O1s and (E) Si 2p, respectively. (F) FL excitation spectrum (a), emission spectrum (b) and UV–vis absorption spectra of Si NPs (c). Inset photographs are Si NPs solutions under visible light (A) and UV light (B) illumination.

(A) Normalized FL intensity of Si NPs at different pH values. (B) Normalized FL intensity of the Si NPs in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4) with various concentrations of NaCl. (C) FL intensity of Si NPs as function of time under 570 nm light illumination. (D) Normalized FL intensity of Si NPs after incubation at different temperature. The error bars stand for the standard deviation of three measurements.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of Si NPs (pink line), Si NPs in the presence of L-Met (blue line), Si NPs in the presence of Cu2+ (black line) and Si NPs in the presence of both Cu2+ and L-Met (red line). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of the Si NPs at various excitation wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) FL emission spectra of Si NPs upon addition of different concentrations of Cu2+ (from top to bottom, 0, 0.03, 0.1, 1, 3, 5,10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 50, 60, 80, 100, 130, 150 and 180 μM, respectively) in 10 mM PBS solution (pH 7.4). (B) Linear calibration plot between FL quenching efficiency ((F 0–F)/F 0) and the concentration of Cu2+ (0.03–35 μM). The error bars stand for the standard deviation of three measurements.

(A) FL emission spectra of Si NPs + Cu2+ aqueous solution with the gradual addition of L-Met (from bottom to top, 0, 0.1, 2, 4, 7, 13, 17, 22, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 60, 80, 100, 120, 150, 180, 200, 220 and 240 μM, respectively) in 10 mM PBS solution (pH 7.4). (B) Linear calibration plot of the FL intensity of Si NPs-Cu2+ ((F–F 0)/F) against the concentration of L-Met (0.1–45 μM). The error bars stand for the standard deviation of three measurements.

(A) Normalized FL intensity of the Si NPs toward Cu2+ (30 μM) and other metal ions (10-fold concentration of Cu2+). (B) Normalized FL intensity of the Si NPs + Cu2+ system towards L-Met (45 μM) and other amino acids (10-fold concentration of Met). All experiments were performed in 10 mM PBS buffer solutions (pH 7.4). The error bars stand for the standard deviation of three measurements.

(A) FL excitation spectrum (pink) and emission spectrum (red) of Si NPs, and UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cu2+ (blue). (B) Stern-Volmer plot of Si NPs solution upon the addition of different concentrations of Cu2+ (0.7, 5, 15 and 33 μM). (C) Lifetimes of steady-state FL of Si NPs and Si NPs + Cu2+. (D) Zeta potential histogram of Si NPs and Si NPs + Cu2+. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(A) The synthetic approach of Si NPs; (B) schematic illustration of Si NPs for Cu2+ and L-Met detection.
The quenching constants (K q) between Cu2+ and Si NPs calculated by Stern-Volmer equation.
Analytical results of L-Met detection in urine samples.
Analytical results of Cu2+ detection in water samples.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of orange-emissive silicon nanoparticles as “off-on” fluorescence probe for sensitive and selective detection of l-methionine and copper

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources, Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), Lanzhou, 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources
Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources, Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), Lanzhou, 730000, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs) are of great interest as they are free of heavy ions. However, most of Si NPs exhibit blue or green emission, while orange or red-emitting Si NPs are required for an extensive range of applications. Copper ion (Cu2+) and l
-methionine (L-Met) detection is critically valuable point since their abnormal level is an indicator of various diseases. In this work, we illustrate an “off-on” method for sensitively and selectively determination of Cu2+ and L-Met using Si NPs as fluorescent probe. The Si NPs emitting orange fluorescence with the quantum yield of 2.23% were prepared via one and easy step of hydrothermal treatment of 3(2-aminoethylamino) propyl (dimethoxymethylsilane) (AEAPDMMS) and 2–aminophenol as precursors. The fluorescence of Si NPs was quenched in the presence of Cu2+ due to the strong metal-ligand coordination and electrostatic interactions between the large amount of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of Si NPs and Cu2+. Surprisingly, the resulted non-fluorescent Si NPs-Cu2+ complex displayed a fluorescence “turn-on” toward L-Met, due to the competitive coordination of Cu2+ between L-Met and Si NPs which leads to the unique “off-on” response to L-Met after the release of free Si NPs. The as-proposed approach is fast, simple, low cost and environmental-friendly. More importantly, it has been applied in the determination of Cu2+ and L-Met in water and urine samples, respectively with satisfactory recoveries. Furthermore, the approach could detect Cu2+ and L-Met with detection limit of 0.012 μM and 0.07 μM, which are lower than the level of Cu2+ in drinking water and of L-Met in human urine sample (maximum ~20 μM and ~5.9 μM, respectively).

ce:section.ce:para: Determination of biological/chemical molecules with high sensitivity is crucial for diseases treatment and prevention [1]. Recently, thiol containing amino acids and important cations has attracted great attention owing to their extensive range of biological processes [2,3]. The specific prominence has been attributed to Cu2+ as the third mostly available transition metal ions in the human body after iron and zinc. Cu2+ ion is commonly available in nature and enter the human body via drinking water. According to the guidelines of World Health Organization (WHO), the admissible level of Cu2+ in drinking water is ~20 μM [1]. Cu2+ plays a valuable role in various biological reactions especially in the electron transfer processes [4]. The shortage of Cu2+ in the body inhibits the cell metabolism and affects enzyme activity, causing various neurological problems while its overabundance in the body damages both kidneys and liver [5]. On the other hand, together with cysteine, L-Met is one of two thiol-containing proteinogenic amino acids [6,7]. It plays a crucial role in prevention of skin and hair disorders and constitutes the main supply of sulfur in the diet [8,9]. L-Met is essential for human growth and increases lecithin production in liver which leads to the reduction of cholesterol level [10,11]. According to European Union (EU), the level of Met in human urine is ~5.9 μM [12]. Abnormal conversion of Met leads to atherosclerosis and its shortage have been assigned to impaired growth, depression, muscle paralysis and toxemia [13,14]. Therefore, in view of the significance attributed to Cu2+ and L-Met, their selective and sensitive determination is very important in the clinical point of view.

Nanomaterials based sensors have attracted tremendous attention due to their valuable role in extensive range of fields such as nanotechnology, medicine, biology, pharmacology and chemistry [15]. Recently, appreciable efforts have been attributed to the synthesis of nanosensors especially CdTe QDs [16], Ag NPs [17], CdS QDs [18] and carbon nanomaterials [19,20] due to their plasmonic bonds and chemical stability, allowing their substantial advancement [21]. However, metal-based nanosensors have revealed various disadvantages including high toxicity and cost, which affect their commercialization and wide spread application. Therefore, the synthesis of novel environmentally friendly nanomaterials to compensate the shortcoming of metal–based nanosensors is still an utmost urgency. Silicon, the second mostly available element on the earth, is crucial to human health due to its property of nontoxicity or low toxicity [22]. Silicon-based nanostructures have attracted a number of researches due to its good water solubility, strong photostability, favorable biocompatibility and low toxicity [23]. In past few years, the sensing applications, structural properties and synthetic approaches of fluorescent silicon-based nanosensors family have gain much attention [24]. Various reports on blue-emitting silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs) have been published [25–27] and the quantity of studies related to green and yellow-emitting Si NPs has recently increased [28–30]. However, there have been no report on orange or red-emitting Si NPs synthesized without the requirements of additional surface modifications. Various aspects have to be considered for the synthesis of Si NPs with longer emission wavelengths. First, a good dispersion of Si NPs in high polarity solvent is required [31]. Second, applicable synthetic parameters including the reaction temperature and time should be considered [32]. Third, a suitable silicon source incorporating heteroatoms and aromatic rings is required [33]. And finally, the properties of Si NPs have to be tuned for achieving an increased high degree of surface oxidation [34], increased π-conjugation length [35] and an increased particle size [34].

In the present work, a novel “off-on” sensing approach for Cu2+ and L-Met using fluorescent Si NPs was designed. Orange-emitting fluorescent Si NPs have been synthesized for the first time via a simple and fast one-step of hydrothermal treatment using AEAPDMMS as silicon nitrogen-rich source and 2-aminophenol as reduction reagent (see scheme 1 A). Most importantly, the prepared Si NPs have been achieved without any doping or functionalization step. 2-aminophenol was selected as a reducing agent for Si NPs synthesis since the π-π* transition of CC bond in 2-aminophenol could enhance the fluorescence (FL) emission of the prepared Si NPs. Compared to the previously reported methods for Si NPs synthesis, the present method is time-saving, simple and did not require high temperature processing [36,37]. Additionally, the prepared Si NPs exhibited the salt tolerability, photobleaching resistance and conspicuous water solubility which therefore extend their potential applications. A large amount of amino (-NH2) and hydroxyl (-OH) groups were recognized on the surface of Si NPs, supplying metal ions coordinate points. As illustrated in scheme 1B, the FL emitted by Si NPs could be selectively quenched by Cu2+ and the integrated system of Cu2+-Si NPs was employed as a non-fluorescent probe for L-Met determination with high sensitivity and selectivity. Within the introduction of L-Met, the strong coordination between Cu2+ and L-Met weakened the interaction between Cu2+ and Si NPs, leading to the FL turn-on of Si NPs.

With the assistance of microwave radiations, high pressure or temperature, AEAPDMMS as a silane coupling agent bearing two amino groups can be reduced by several reductants including ethylene glycol, hydroquinone and catechol to produce N-doped Si NPs [24,37,38]. In this work, for the first time, we designed a more gentle and convenient synthetic protocol for AEAPDMMS-evolved Si NPs with the assistance of 2-aminophenol. Orange fluorescent Si NPs were obtained through the following experimental protocol. Appropriately, to 5.0 mL of deionized water, 10.0 mg of 2-aminophenol was added and mixed at room temperature by continuous stirring (1200 r/min) in a water bath to obtain the uniformly dispersed solution. Afterwards, 1.0 mL of AEAPDMMS was added dropwise to the obtained mixture and the reaction temperature was raised at 120 °C for 20 min. For the purification of the obtained Si NPs, a dialysis membrane (1000 Da, molecular weight cutoff) was used for 24h. Later on, the purified Si NPs were stored in a 4 °C refrigerator for future uses. In addition, a part of purified Si NPs solution was freeze-dried, crushed and grinded to get the fine powder of Si NPs for XPS, FT-IR and XRD characterization.

The FL quenching experiment of Si NPs by Cu2+ was performed by preparing 10 mM of Cu2+ as the stock solution. Working solutions of Cu2+ (0.03–180 μM) were prepared by diluting the stock solution by phosphate buffered saline (PBS) as buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.4). For performing the quenching experiment, PBS buffer solution was added to 30 μL of Si NPs and then, different concentrations of Cu2+ were dispersed into the prepared solution (Si NPs + PBS). Before measuring the FL signals, the prepared mixture was incubated at room temperature for 3 min. Emission signals were recorded by fixing the excitation wavelength at 470 nm. Various metal ions were evaluated to conduct the selectivity experiment towards Cu2+ (Na+, K+, Ag+, Zn+, Ni+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ba2+ and Fe3+) and were measured under similar operation procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensor preparation and its application for L-Met detection
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing system (Si NPs-Cu2+) was designed by adding 180 μM Cu2+ to 30 μL Si NPs in PBS buffer solution for the complex formation. The formed complex revealed a very weak FL and was then used as detector for L-Met sensing. Various concentrations of L-Met (0–240 μM) were dispersed into the sensing system (Si NPs-Cu2+) and their corresponding FL signals were measured under excitation wavelength at 470 nm, after incubation of the mixture for 1 min. For the selectivity measurement towards L-Met, glutathione, alanine, arginine, histidine, glycine, serine, cysteine, valine, lysine, proline, threonine, leucine, trypsin, asparagine, and trypsin were separately introduced to the sensing system (Si NPs-Cu2+) via the optimal conditions. Experiments were recorded by triplicate for selectivity and sensitivity measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: L-Met and Cu2+ determination in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the feasibility of this method for both Cu2+ and L-Met, water samples (Yellow River water and drinking water) and Human urine samples were selected and pre-treated. Yellow River water sample was received from Lanzhou (Gansu, China). The large particles in Yellow River water were removed by centrifugation at 1200 rpm for 10 min and filtration through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The collected water was then kept for further use. The used drinking water sample was acquired from one of the supermarkets in Lanzhou (Gansu, China). Human urine samples were received from healthy volunteers and no further pre-treatment were applied to them except the dilution with 10.0 mM PBS buffer solution (pH 7.4). The emission spectra for Cu2+ and L-Met determination were recorded under excitation wavelength at 470 nm after incubation of 3 min and 1 min at room temperature, respectively. The standard additional method was used to validate the accuracy of the method. Relative recoveries were figured out by spiking 5 and 10 μM for both Cu2+ and L-Met and were adjusted using the following formula:
R e c o v e r y
( % )
=
F o u n d
v a l u e
A d d e d
v a l u e
× 100 %

Morphology and size of the synthesized Si NPs were characterized by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As illustrated in Fig. 1 A, the obtained Si NPs were regular in shape and well dispersed. The size distribution histogram corresponding to the prepared Si NP (inset in Fig. 1A) revealed that the particles size was ranged from 1.8 to 3.4 nm, with the average diameter of 2.7 nm. As confirmed by XRD analysis of the prepared Si NPs (figure S2), a broad peak centered at 2.4 Å was recognized. This implies that Si NPs were in amorphous state [39]. C, N, O and Si elements were confirmed by EDX pattern as the components of Si NPs (figure S4). The functional groups presented on the surface of Si NPs were investigated using FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1B). Two peaks at 693 and 746 cm−1 were attributed to N–H secondary amine vibration and wagging vibration, while the stretching peak at 933 cm−1 was attributed to Si–O–H group which involved a strong peak of Si–O [40]. The band at 1028-1130 cm−1 and the peak at 1277 cm−1 were assigned to Si–O–Si and C–O stretching vibrations respectively [41]. Absorption peaks at 1348 and 1410 cm−1 belonged to C–N stretching vibration and bending vibration respectively [42]. The signal at 1602 cm−1 was assigned to the bending vibration of N–H and 2 peaks at 1470 and 2931 cm−1 were pertained to the bending vibration and unsaturated stretching vibration of C–H band [43]. In addition, the absorption bands at 3270-3352 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibrations of N–H and in-plane deformation vibration of O–H bond [44]. FTIR spectrum has proved that the surface of Si NPs was rich in hydrophilic groups especially –NH2 and –OH, which empower Si NPs with high stability and aqueous dispersibility. Additionally, the availability of nitrogen element can definitely regulate the photic properties and surface state of nanomaterials, which end-up by production of better luminescent properties [45]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments illustrated more satisfying evidences confirming for the chemical composition of Si NPs. 5 major peaks were illustrated in Fig. 2 A at 102.2, 153.7, 284.3, 399.3 and 531.4 eV in the full-scan of XPS spectrum and were attributed to Si 2p, Si 2s, C1s, N1s and O1s. The high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s exhibited 4 types of carbon atoms at 284.1 eV (C–Si), 284.6 eV (C–C/CC), 285.2eV (C–N) and at 285.7 eV (C–O) (Fig. 2B) [44]. Three dominant peaks at 398.3, 399.0 and 399.5 eV were observed on XPS spectrum of N1s (Fig. 2C) corresponding to N–Si, N–C and Si–N–O [23]. Three fitted peaks were observed on XPS spectrum of O1s (Fig. 2D). The peak at 532.4 eV was attributed to C–OH/C–O–C and two peaks at 531.7 and 530.7 eV were assigned to Si–O [46]. XPS spectrum of Si 2p illustrated three peaks centered at 101.4, 102.0 and 102.5 eV corresponding to Si–C, Si–N and Si–O respectively (Fig. 2E) [44]. XPS determination on the surface constitution of Si NPs and the FTIR results were mutually compatible. FL spectrum and UV–vis absorption were conducted to confirm optical properties of Si NPs. As illustrated in Fig. 2F (curve c), peaks at > 350 nm and ~280 nm were assigned to n-π* transition of CN or C–O and π-π* transition of CC, respectively. The wide absorption band ~240 nm was attributed to n-σ* transition of C–N or C–O [24]. The prepared Si NPs emitted orange FL (inset of Fig. 2F) and besides, FL spectra of the prepared Si NPs under various excitation wavelength was shown in (Fig. 4B). Si NPs revealed obvious excitation-dependent emission behavior. Obviously, by raising a serial excitation wavelength from 350 nm to 530 nm, the FL emission wavelength red-shifted from 480 nm to 590 nm and up to 470 nm, the FL spectra gradually decreased and from there, 470 and 570 nm were selected as the maximum excitation and emission wavelength, respectively (Fig. 2F, curve a and b). Such excitation dependent-independent emission behaviors of Si NPs could be assigned to the oxygen induced defects and the direct bandgap transition, respectively [47]. Moreover, such feature of excitation dependent of the FL emitted by Si NPs gives a great promise for the prospective multicolor application in the FL imaging experiment [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthetic condition of Si NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Si NPs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment of 2-aminophenol and AEAPDMMS as both surface functionalizing and silicon source agents. The best FL intensities were obtained after optimization of synthetic parameters including three aspects: The ratio of AEAPDMMS to 2-aminophenol, the reaction temperature and the reaction time. The molar ratio investigation was performed by fixing 1.0 mL as the volume of AEAPDMMS and changing the amount of 2-aminophenol. As observed on figure S1A, the FL intensity increased as amount of 2 aminophenol was increasing and up to 10 mg, a slight decreasing was observed. Effects of reaction temperature and time on the FL intensity were illustrated on figure S1B and C. It increased up to 120 °C and 20 min and then decreased sequentially. In summary, the optimized synthetic parameters were selected as the ratio of 10 mg 2-aminophenol/1.0 mL AEAPDMMS, reaction temperature of 120 °C and reaction time of 20 min for further studies.

The stability of nanosensor deals with the degree to which sensor characteristics remain constant over some period of time depending on the sensing medium. Meanwhile, the robust stability of nanosensors will be conducive to their potential biological applications. However, changes in stability leads to variations in the signal to noise ratio which end up by decreasing the sensitivity of the probe towards the analyte [47,49]. Based on above explanations, we investigated the stability of our synthesized Si NPs for their convenient biosensing application. Particularly, the FL intensity of Si NPs revealed a gradual decrease when the pH was raised from 3.0 to 7.0 and was almost stable at the pH ranging from 7.4 to 12 (Fig. 3 A). This was due to the protonation/deprotonation as result of the functional groups available on the surface of Si NPs [24]. Furthermore, the FL intensity of Si NPs is insensitive to ionic strength even at high concentration of Na Cl (Fig. 3B). The FL of Si NPs maintains its initial intensity after 60 min of UV irradiation (Fig. 3C). This suggests an impressive photostability of the prepared Si NPs. Besides, aqueous solution of Si NPs preserves its strong FL in wide range of temperature raising from 20 °C to 100 °C (Fig. 3D). Such prominent stability in temperature could be assigned to the sufficient surface-covered –NH2/-OH groups, serving as defensive shells around nanoparticles [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of Si NPs towards Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: The FL intensity of Si NPs was quenched after the introduction of various concentrations of Cu2+. Such sensitivity was studied after optimization of reaction time. The FL intensities of Si NPs at 570 nm were recorded in the time ranging from 0 to 5 min and thus, two various Cu2+ concentrations (10 and 20 μM) were investigated and all illustrated almost the same results (figure S3A) and therefore, 3 min was chosen as optimum reaction time. Various concentrations of Cu2+ (0–180 μM) were prepared and used to analyze their effect on the FL intensity of Si NPs. As shown on Fig. 5 A, there was a remarkable decrease of the FL intensity upon increasing the concentration of Cu2+. Through the quenching efficiencies (F 0–F)/F 0 and Cu2+ concentrations, a good linear relationship was remarked in the concentrations range from 0.03 to 35 μM, and a good linear correlation coefficient (R 2 = 0.9984) was observed (Fig. 5B). (F 0 and F were FL intensities of Si NPs at 570 nm before and after introduction of Cu2+, respectively). The calculated limit of detection (LOD) was 0.012 μM, which was obtained based on s/k = 3 (k is the slope of the calibration curve and s stands for the standard deviation of the blank solution). As shown in table S2, the calculated LOD was lower than other reported results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Si NPs-Cu2+ as fluorescence turn-on sensor for L-Met
ce:section.ce:para: It is described that Cu2+ and L-Met could react due to the possession of thiol group attributed to Met [2]. Thus, Si NPs-Cu2+ complex can be investigated to monitor SH-containing amino acids due to the competitive behavior between Si NPs, Cu2+ and SH-containing amino acids. As illustrated on Fig. 4A, the FL of Si NPs could be quenched after addition of 180 μM Cu2+. This is due to the formation of stable complex between Si NPs and Cu2+ (Si NPs-Cu2+). Fig. 6 A shows the FL signals of Si NPs-Cu2+ complex in the presence of various concentrations of L-Met. Such sensitivity was investigated after optimization of reaction time. The FL intensities of Si NPs at 570 nm were recorded in the time range from 0 to 4 min and thus, two various L-Met concentrations (20 and 40 μM) were studied and all revealed the same results (figure S3B) and therefore, 1 min was chosen as optimum reaction time. After optimizing the reaction time, at the addition of approximately low L-Met, the FL was quenched till around zero. The FL signal increases with raising L-Met concentration till 240 μM, confirming the FL “turn-on” response. The process of FL “off-on” state is described as follow: generally, the addition of L-Met brought a novel competition with Si NPs to bind Cu2+. At relatively low L-Met concentration, L-Met partly replaced Si NPs in the Si NPs-Cu2+ complex as result of its excellent attraction with Cu2+ comparing to Si NPs and thus, this leads to the formation of Met-Cu2+- Si NPs complex. Finally, Met-Cu2+-Si NPs was completely converted into non-fluorescent (Cu2+-Met) complex. Concurrently, Si NPs was also discharged into buffer solution, ending up by the FL enhancement. Once all Met-Cu2+-Si NPs complex was completely converted to Met-Cu2+ complex, the FL intensity was raised closer to its initial value (Si NPs signal without Cu2+ and L-Met). Above mechanism confirms the FL on-state to L-Met. Therefore, the introduction of L-Met not only serves as a shelter to Si NPs from being quenched, but also restored the FL owing to its possession of thiol group capable of removing Cu2+ absorbed on the surface of Si NPs. As illustrated on Fig. 6A, the FL intensity increased as L-Met concentration was increasing and via the enhancing efficiencies (F–F 0)/F and L-Met concentrations, a good linear relationship could be remarked in the concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 45 μM with a good linear correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.98877 (Fig. 6B). F 0 and F were FL intensities of Si NPs-Cu2+ complex at 570 nm in the absence and presence of L-Met, respectively. Detection limit was calculated as 0.07 μM, and was obtained based on the above-mentioned formula (s/k = 3). As illustrated in table S3, the calculated LOD is lower than some other reported literatures for Met detection.

Selectivity has been one of greatest challenges for Cu2+ and L-Met sensing in biological samples. In this work, the effect of other metal ions (Na+, K+, Ag+, Zn+, Ni+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ba2+ and Fe3+) on the FL intensity of Si NPs was investigated. As illustrated on Fig. 7 A, only Cu2+ revealed the capability of quenching the FL of Si NPs even at 10-fold concentration of interfering agents. The selectivity of Si NPs-Cu2+ towards L-Met was also studied by measuring its FL intensity at 470 nm, comparing to other amino acids (glutathione, alanine, arginine, histidine, glycine, serine, cysteine, valine, lysine, proline, threonine, leucine, trypsin, asparagine, and trypsin). As shown on Fig. 7B, even the introduction of other thiol containing amino acids (cysteine and GSH) could not allow the decomposition of formed Si NPs-Cu2+ complex and lead to the variations in the FL intensity, and only L-Met remarked an obvious increase of the FL. Moreover, the introduction of above-mentioned amino acids did not disturb the measurement of Si NPs-Cu2+ towards L-Met, even at 10-fold concentration. This confirms the feasibility of our proposed approach for L-Met sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: L-Met and Cu2+determination in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para:
Due to excellent selectivity and sensitivity of the method, the designed approach was applied to measure the amount of Cu2+ and L-Met in water and human urine samples, respectively. As it can be seen in Tables 2 and 3
, the average recoveries for L-Met in urine samples and for Cu2+ in water samples reached 93.4–101.4% and 97.6–107.0%, respectively. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 5%, which is tolerable for quantitative analysis conducted in biological samples. This confirms that the designed approach revealed a superb potential applicability.

Generally, the FL emitted by luminescent materials could be quenched due to four known quenching effects: inner filter effect (IFE), Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET), dynamic quenching effect (DQE) and static quenching effect (SQE) [43]. When the fluorophore forms a stable complex with another molecule, it is thus said to be statically quenched (SQE). The FL is said to be dynamically quenched (DQE), when the FL of a luminescent material is lost and go back to the ground state, due to the collision between the quencher and the fluorophore [51]. When an overlapping is observed between an excitation or emission spectrum of the luminescent material and UV–vis absorption spectrum of the quencher, the quenching is suggested to stem from FRET or IFE [37]. Metal ions could quench the FL of Si NPs through the complicated pathway; thus, various procedures were investigated to demonstrate possible means in which the FL of our prepared Si NPs could be quenched by Cu2+ [52]. As shown on Fig. 8 A, no overlapping was observed between the FL excitation/emission spectrum of Si NPs and the absorption spectra of the quencher (Cu2+). Such observation directly excluded the existence of FRET or IFE. In addition, the quenching results were also interpreted via the Stern-Volmer plot. Since both DQE and SQE fit to the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (1)), the effect of temperature dependance was investigated [53].
(1)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K q
τ 0
[ C ]
= 1 +
K sv
[ C ]
Where K q is the FL quenching rate constant which reflects effects of inter-diffusion and inter-collision in this system. F and F 0 refer to the steady-state FL intensities of the quencher and K sv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. [C] is the concentration of the quencher.
(2)
K q
=
K sv
/
τ 0
Where τ 0 refers to the average lifetime of the Si NPs in the absence of Cu2+. The average FL lifetime of the prepared Si NPs in our work is 3.62 ns (table S1). Conforming to Eq. (2), K q values were calculated as 8.23 × 1012, 7.65 × 1012 and 7.12 × 1012 L mol−1 s−1, with their corresponding temperatures at 293, 303 and 313 K (Table 1). When the plot of F 0/F versus [C] in a certain concentration range gives an ascending curvature, the quenching could be assigned to single static or dynamic [43]. In this work, as observed on Fig. 8B, there is a good linear relationship between Cu2+ concentrations (0.7, 5, 15 and 33 μM) and F 0/F. As shown in Table 1, as the temperature increased, K q and K sv (slope) decreased. All obtained K q values were greater than the maximum dynamic K q (2.0 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1) which is known as the maximum diffusion limited rate. This is obtained via the summarized series of experimental results of the diffusion procedure by Ware's group [53]. When the calculated K q is much greater than the maximum dynamic K q, the quenching is suggested to be caused by SQE [54]. Results obtained from our work confirmed that the FL quenching was from SQE not from the dynamic collision [43]. Moreover, the FL lifetime of Si NPs in the presence and absence of different concentrations of Cu2+ was studied for in-depth investigation of which mechanism is responsible for the FL quenching of Si NPs. As observed on Fig. 8C and table S1, the FL lifetime of Si NPs did not change in the presence and absence of different concentrations of Cu2+. This suggests that the FL of Si NPs was statically quenched by Cu2+ (SQE) [49]. In addition, the interaction between Si NPs and Cu2+ was confirmed by the zeta potential experiment of Si NPs in the absence and presence of Cu2+, which increased from −4.31 to 2.12 mV, respectively (Fig. 8D) [55]. Fig. 4A reveals the fluorescence intensity of Si NPs (pink line), Si NPs in the presence of L-Met (blue line), Si NPs in the presence of Cu2+ (black line) and Si NPs in the presence of both Cu2+ and L-Met (red line).

We have successfully illustrated a simple and fast synthetic procedure to prepare orange-emitting fluorescent Si NPs by one and easy step of hydrothermal treatment. The resulted Si NPs exhibit high photostability and water-solubility. Cu2+ can quench the FL of Si NPs due to the electrostatic interactions between amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of Si NPs and Cu2+. More importantly, due to the competitive coordination of Cu2+ between L-Met and Si NPs, thiol group of L-Met could bind Cu2+ and remove it from the surface of Si NPs, leading to the FL recovery. Therefore, the FL “off-on” response for straightforward determination of Cu2+ and L-Met was developed. Furthermore, the proposed method was successfully applied in the determination of Cu2+ in water samples and L-Met in urine sample. The study also opens an avenue for the synthesis of Si NPs holding great promise for potential applications such as cell imaging, biocatalyst and nanosensors. However, improving Si NPs’ FL to achieve longer emission wavelength is still a major target.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21705156 and 81673325), Outstanding Youth Fund of Gansu Province (20JR10RA061), CAS "Light of West China" Program, CAS Pioneer Hundred Talents Program, and the Chinese Academy of Sciences-the World Academy of Sciences (CAS-TWAS) President's Fellowship Program.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2021.122369.

Fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs) are of great interest as they are free of heavy ions. However, most of Si NPs exhibit blue or green emission, while orange or red-emitting Si NPs are required for an extensive range of applications. Copper ion (Cu2+) and methionine (Met) detection is critically valuable point since their abnormal level is an indicator of various diseases. In this work, we illustrate an “off-on” method for sensitively and selectively determination of Cu2+ and Met using Si NPs as fluorescent probe. The Si NPs emitting orange fluorescence with the quantum yield of 2.23% were prepared via one and easy step of hydrothermal treatment of 3(2-aminoethylamino) propyl (dimethoxymethylsilane) (AEAPDMMS) and 2–aminophenol as precursors. The fluorescence of Si NPs was quenched in the presence of Cu2+ due to the strong metal-ligand coordination and electrostatic interactions between the large amount of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of Si NPs and Cu2+. Surprisingly, the resulted non-fluorescent Si NPs-Cu2+ complex displayed a fluorescence “turn-on” toward Met, due to the competitive coordination of Cu2+ between Met and Si NPs which leads to the unique “off-on” response to Met after the release of free Si NPs. The as-proposed approach is fast, simple, low cost and environmental-friendly. More importantly, it has been applied in the determination of Cu2+ and Met in water and urine samples, respectively with satisfactory recoveries. Furthermore, the approach could detect Cu2+ and Met with detection limit of 0.012 μM and 0.07 μM, which are lower than the level of Cu2+ in drinking water and of Met in human urine sample (maximum ~20 μM and ~5.9 μM, respectively).
